evidence: gateway-client.ts captures GATEWAY_INTERNAL_URL (line 11-14) and INTERNAL_API_SECRET (line 15) at module scope as frozen constants, while internal-auth.ts reads process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET at call time (line 20). Same env var, two strategies — tests that mutate process.env after import cannot affect gateway-client.

env.ts line 50 performs eager Zod validation at import time (serverEnvSchema.parse(process.env)), which would throw during import if env vars are missing. However, this module is currently dead code — no file imports serverEnv.

db.ts uses the standard globalThis singleton pattern for PrismaClient (lines 4-19). This is a well-known Next.js pattern but creates a true global singleton with implicit creation ordering — any module importing prisma triggers DB client instantiation.

socket.ts uses module-scoped mutable variables (let socket, let currentToken at lines 5-6) as a client-side singleton. Access is gated behind getSocket() which is lazy and guarded by typeof window check. This is a reasonable pattern for client-side singletons.

rate-limit.ts exports pre-instantiated RateLimiter singletons at module scope (authLimiter, webhookLimiter at lines 98-101), each of which starts a setInterval timer at construction time. Importing the module triggers timer creation as a side effect.
middleware.ts instantiates its own loginLimiter at module scope (line 7), separate from the shared instances in rate-limit.ts — three distinct RateLimiter singletons exist across the codebase.

Test files (agent-handlers.test.ts line 37, route-handlers.test.ts line 49, typed-messages.test.ts line 66) capture process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET at module scope as ORIGINAL_SECRET. Since gateway-client.ts also captures at module scope, restoring env in afterEach does not restore gateway-client's stale copy.
auth.ts reads process.env.NEXTAUTH_URL and NEXTAUTH_SECRET at module scope within the authOptions object literal (lines 108, 128), but this is standard NextAuth bootstrap.

strengths: encryption.ts correctly uses a getKey() function to read ENCRYPTION_KEY at call time rather than module scope, providing a good pattern to follow
internal-auth.ts reads process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET at call time inside validateInternalSecret(), making it test-friendly and resilient to env changes
agent-factory.ts buildConnectionInfo() reads NEXT_PUBLIC_GATEWAY_URL and NEXTAUTH_URL at call time, not import time
socket.ts client singleton uses lazy initialization gated behind getSocket() with a typeof window guard, avoiding SSR import-time side effects
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The codebase has an inconsistent strategy for environment variable access — some modules correctly defer reads to call time while others snapshot at import time, creating test fragility and a split personality around the same secrets.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates awareness of initialization concerns (lazy getKey(), call-time env reads in auth modules, standard Prisma singleton pattern) but gateway-client.ts captures two critical env vars at module scope, contradicting the pattern used by its sibling internal-auth.ts for the same INTERNAL_API_SECRET. Module-scope RateLimiter instantiation with setInterval timers is a minor side-effect concern. The inconsistency is real but contained to a few files, and the overall architecture avoids circular init dependencies or complex boot-order requirements. Score of 72.5 reflects a generally sound approach with a notable inconsistency in a critical infrastructure module.

issues.summary: gateway-client.ts captures INTERNAL_API_SECRET and GATEWAY_INTERNAL_URL at import time, contradicting the call-time pattern used by internal-auth.ts for the same variable
evidence: gateway-client.ts line 15: const INTERNAL_API_SECRET = process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET; (module scope)
internal-auth.ts line 20: const expected = process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET; (inside function body)
gateway-client.ts lines 11-14: const GATEWAY_INTERNAL_URL = process.env.GATEWAY_INTERNAL_URL || ... (module scope)
Tests set process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET in beforeEach but gateway-client already captured a stale value at import time
issues.suggestion: Move env reads inside the functions that use them, matching internal-auth.ts pattern. Replace module-level const with a helper like getGatewayInternalUrl() and getInternalApiSecret(), or read process.env directly at each call site.

issues.summary: RateLimiter constructor starts a setInterval timer as a side effect; three instances are created at import time across two files
evidence: rate-limit.ts line 31: setInterval(() => this.cleanup(), 60_000) runs in the constructor
rate-limit.ts lines 98-101: authLimiter and webhookLimiter instantiated at module scope
middleware.ts line 7: loginLimiter instantiated at module scope
Any import of rate-limit.ts triggers timer creation even if the limiter is never used
issues.suggestion: Defer timer creation to first check() call using a lazy init flag, or export factory functions instead of pre-instantiated singletons. This avoids timer side effects in test environments and unused imports.

issues.summary: Test files capture process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET at module scope, but this cannot restore the stale snapshot held by gateway-client.ts
evidence: agent-handlers.test.ts line 37: const ORIGINAL_SECRET = process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET; captured at module scope
Tests modify process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET in beforeEach/afterEach but gateway-client.ts line 15 already holds a frozen copy from import time
This creates a hidden coupling: test isolation depends on gateway-client not being imported in the same test module
issues.suggestion: Fix the root cause (gateway-client import-time capture). For tests, use vi.mock or vi.stubEnv to ensure all env reads are intercepted, or restructure gateway-client to read env at call time.

issues.summary: env.ts eagerly parses all server env vars at import time via Zod, but the export is never imported — latent bomb if anyone imports it
evidence: env.ts line 50: export const serverEnv = serverEnvSchema.parse(process.env); runs at import time
Zod parse will throw immediately if any required env var is missing (DATABASE_URL, REDIS_URL, NEXTAUTH_SECRET, JWT_SECRET, INTERNAL_API_SECRET, ENCRYPTION_KEY)
No file in the codebase currently imports serverEnv — the module is dead code but remains a trap for future imports
issues.suggestion: Either convert serverEnv to a lazy getter function (matching the existing getClientEnv() pattern on line 53), or remove the dead module. The getClientEnv() function in the same file already demonstrates the correct lazy approach.

root_causes: Inconsistent convention for environment variable access: some modules use import-time capture, others use call-time reads, with no documented policy choosing between them
RateLimiter class couples construction with timer creation, making it impossible to import without side effects
likely_symptoms: test_env_snapshot_stale_restore is a downstream symptom of gateway_client_import_time_env_capture
possible_false_positives: env_ts_eager_parse_dead_code is currently inert since no file imports it — it is a latent risk rather than an active defect
